problem_summary: 2025 MCM/ICM Problem B (Sustainable Tourism), an open-ended policy/discrete-systems problem set in Juneau, Alaska, a town of ~30,000 hit by overtourism (1.6M cruise passengers/year). Teams had to build a model that (1) balances tourism's economic benefits against environmental and social costs, recommends how to spend the extra tax revenue, and feeds those expenditures back into the system; (2) provides sensitivity analysis identifying key factors; (3) demonstrates transferability to another over-touristed destination and to low-traffic attractions; and (4) writes a memo to the tourism council. This O-award paper (team 2504448) frames it as a multi-year sequential policy-optimization problem and solves it with a multi-objective dynamic program over a 5-year horizon, then re-instantiates the same machinery for Miami.
models_used: Multi-objective finite-horizon DYNAMIC PROGRAMMING (Bellman) as the master model: state = (tourist count T, environmental index E, resident satisfaction Q); controls/decision variables = tax rate tau(t) and government expenditure M(t); value function V(t)=max over (tau,M) of {P(t)+w4*E(t)+w5*Q(t)+gamma*V(t+1)}, solved by BACKWARD RECURSION over a 5-year horizon (2024-2028)

Tourist DEMAND model: discrete-time multiplicative recurrence T(t)=T(t-1)*(1+g(t)-gamma*tau(t)+delta(t)) with a tax-elasticity term gamma, stochastic demand shocks delta(t), and an endogenous growth rate g(t)=g_base - beta*C_cap(t) + eta*M(t) coupling visitor caps and promotion spend to growth
SEASONAL decomposition: T split into peak/off-peak via multiplicative factors S_peak>1, S_off<1
ECONOMIC model: linear revenue R(t)=r*T(t), tax G(t)=tau*R(t), cost C(t)=c_fixed+c_variable*T(t), profit P=R-C

ENVIRONMENTAL impact model: a composite environmental index built from temperature, ocean temperature, snowfall, ocean pH via min-max normalization + signed weights (+/-1) and ENTROPY WEIGHT METHOD; plus government-intervention boost E_sub=E+alpha*M(t) and a LOGISTIC-style recovery/pressure dynamic E(t+1)=E_sub+r*(1-E_sub)-h*(T/T_max)
RESIDENT SATISFACTION model: Q(t)=w1*(1-|T-T_opt|/T_opt)+w2*E(t)-w3*tau(t), encoding an optimal tourist level T_opt (a tent/penalty function) plus environment and tax-burden terms
Constrained optimization layer: capacity T<=C_max, E>=E_min, Q>=Q_min, budget G>=M
Transfer/adaptation model for Miami: same DP with a CULTURAL INDEX C(t) (also via entropy weighting) added to demand and satisfaction, snow factors swapped for ocean/urban-emission factors

One-way local SENSITIVITY ANALYSIS sweeping average environmental index, average resident satisfaction, and average tourist count against output policy components (tax, infrastructure, subsidy, advertising, environmental protection)
key_techniques: Reframing an open policy prompt as a SEQUENTIAL DECISION PROBLEM and committing to one master method (DP/Bellman) that subsumes three sub-models, so the deliverable is one coherent optimizer rather than three disconnected analyses

Explicit FEEDBACK LOOP between government spending and the rest of the system (M(t) raises growth via eta, raises environment via alpha, and is bounded by tax revenue G) -- directly answering the prompt's 'show how expenditures feed back into the model' requirement
ENTROPY WEIGHT METHOD to derive data-driven weights for the composite environmental and cultural indices, avoiding arbitrary hand-picked weights
Composite index construction via min-max normalization + signed impact weights to fuse heterogeneous units (temperature, pH, snowfall) into one dimensionless quality score
Logistic-type recovery-minus-pressure state transition for environment (natural recovery toward 1 minus per-capita tourist harm) -- a compact way to encode carrying-capacity dynamics
Tent/penalty term 1-|T-T_opt|/T_opt to model that satisfaction peaks at an optimal tourist level and falls off in both directions (over- AND under-tourism)

Decision-relevant sensitivity framing: instead of perturbing parameters and reporting output variance, they sweep state inputs and show how the OPTIMAL POLICY MIX (tax / env-protection / subsidy / advertising shares) responds -- turning sensitivity into a policy-interpretation tool
Transferability by parameter re-instantiation + a structured city-vs-city comparison table (Table 4) that maps each city feature to a concrete model modification
Keeping the model identifiable: deliberately retaining the COVID 2020-2021 zero-tourist outliers as a natural experiment to separate tourism-driven vs. background environmental change
why_it_won: "From a judge's view it wins on FRAMEWORK COHERENCE and full prompt coverage, not on mathematical depth. The DP value function is a single unifying spine that ties economy, environment, and society into one optimizer with explicit decision variables (tax, spending) and constraints -- exactly the 'balance competing objectives + show feedback' structure Problem B rewards. Every sub-question is visibly answered: a concrete recommended policy (dynamic tax 3.2-5.0%, year-by-year expenditure split), a 5-year quantitative forecast (stable ~1.6M visitors, rising env index 0.029->0.181, rising satisfaction 0.882->0.917), a sensitivity section that reads as policy insight rather than a parameter dump, a genuine Miami transfer that adapts (not just copies) the model with a new cultural index, a low-traffic-attraction strategy (dynamic caps, tax differentiation, targeted promotion), and a clean stakeholder memo. The entropy-weight indices give an air of objectivity, and the writing is well-signposted with strong figures and a clear notation table. Judges reward a complete, decision-oriented, communicable story over a brittle 'correct' one, and this paper delivers that end-to-end."
section_highlights: "Summary: dense and self-contained -- states the method (multi-objective DP), the three objectives, and the headline numbers (tax 3.2-5.0%, ~1.6M visitors, rising env & satisfaction) so a judge knows the whole result before page 2. Section 1.3 'Our Work' + flowchart: a roadmap figure that names all four sub-models up front. Section 2: explicit Assumptions list (8 numbered, each justified), a clean Notations table, and a Data Preparation section citing real, named sources (Alaska Travel Industry Assoc., NOAA, CBJ 2023 Tourism Survey). Section 3: each sub-model gets its own equation block with prose justification; the DP section cleanly separates Objective / Constraints / Decision Variables / Solution Approach, which is textbook-clear exposition. Section 3.5.5-3.5.7: a results table plus a year-by-year government-expenditure composition stacked bar -- turns optimizer output into actionable policy. Section 4: three sensitivity sweeps each paired with a plain-English interpretation of which policy lever each input most affects. Section 5: a Juneau-vs-Miami comparison table mapping features to model changes -- the strongest part of the transfer task. Section 6: honest Strengths/Weaknesses. Plus a proper memo and an AI-use appendix."
pitfalls_or_limitations: DP is largely DECORATIVE / under-specified: the paper never states the state-space discretization, the action grid, or how the continuous controls (tau, M) are searched; the 'backward recursion' is asserted but no algorithmic detail or convergence evidence is given, so the optimization is not reproducible

Heavy parameter invention with no calibration: gamma, beta, eta, alpha, h, r, omega_1..5, T_opt, S_peak/off, c_fixed/variable are introduced but never fitted to data or tabulated with values -- results are effectively unfalsifiable

Entropy weighting is mis-stated: Eq (9) treats normalized values x as if they were a probability distribution p_i and the standard 1/ln(n) normalization and column-probability step are missing, so the 'entropy weights' as written are not the textbook method
Linear revenue/cost assumptions (constant revenue per tourist, constant recovery rate) are strong and acknowledged only briefly; no robustness check against non-linear spending or congestion effects

Several FIGURES ARE INTERNALLY INCONSISTENT or corrupt: the 'predictions' bar chart runs 2015-2099 with NaNs and a flat 4.2% tax that contradicts the recommended dynamic 3.2-5.0% schedule; some line charts (e.g. the environmental-index computation figure) show obviously synthetic monotone 0.0-2.2 ramps that don't correspond to real data -- a judge skimming closely would catch these
Validation is weak: 'fitted vs actual tourist count' is shown but no error metric, no out-of-sample test, no comparison to a baseline policy (do-nothing vs optimized)
Sensitivity analysis is one-at-a-time/local only -- no interaction effects, no global (Sobol/Latin-hypercube) analysis
Miami transfer reuses the same hand-set weights with only qualitative justification; no Miami-specific calibration
reusable_patterns: For any 'balance competing objectives + show feedback over time' prompt, default to a FINITE-HORIZON DP / Bellman value function with explicit (state, decision variable, constraint, transition) blocks -- it forces coherence and visibly answers feedback requirements

Build a COMPOSITE INDEX from heterogeneous raw signals via min-max normalization + entropy weighting to convert messy multi-unit data into one defensible, data-driven quality score (environmental, cultural, social) -- reusable across many MCM problems

Make government/policy spending an ENDOGENOUS feedback term that simultaneously enters multiple sub-models (growth, environment) and is budget-constrained -- a clean template for 'how should revenue be reinvested' questions
Encode 'an optimal operating point exists' with a tent/penalty term 1-|x-x_opt|/x_opt so a metric peaks at a target and degrades on both sides (over- and under-use)
Use a recovery-minus-pressure transition E(t+1)=E+r(1-E)-h*(load/capacity) as a compact carrying-capacity dynamic for any renewable-resource state

Demonstrate transferability with a FEATURE->MODEL-CHANGE comparison TABLE: list each destination/scenario feature and the specific parameter or term you alter -- shows adaptation rather than copy-paste and reads convincingly to judges
Convert sensitivity analysis into POLICY INTERPRETATION: sweep each input and report which decision lever it most strongly drives, with a one-sentence takeaway per sweep
Structure the DP write-up as four labeled subsections (Objective, Constraints, Decision Variables, Solution Approach) for instant readability
Retain natural-experiment outliers (e.g. COVID zero-demand years) deliberately to identify background vs. driver effects, and say so
Lead with a numbers-rich Summary and include a roadmap flowchart naming every sub-model so judges grasp scope before reading details
